text: Does adding a controlled nonlinear correction recover real shared signal, or mostly increase noise fitting?
textPreview: Does adding a controlled nonlinear correction recover real shared signal, or mostly increase noise fitting?

text: We compare linear and residual nonlinear encoders on noise-only, linear-signal, and cubic-signal synthetic data.
textPreview: We compare linear and residual nonlinear encoders on noise-only, linear-signal, and cubic-signal synthetic data.

text: Each condition tests a different kind of X/Y relationship while keeping the training protocol fixed.
textPreview: Each condition tests a different kind of X/Y relationship while keeping the training protocol fixed.

text: X and Y are independent Gaussian noise. Any training alignment here is memorization.
textPreview: X and Y are independent Gaussian noise. Any training alignment here is memorization.

text: X has latent Z_x, Y has latent Z_y, with Z_y = Z_x.
textPreview: X has latent Z_x, Y has latent Z_y, with Z_y = Z_x.

text: X has latent Z_x, Y has latent Z_y, with Z_y = standardized(Z_x^3).
textPreview: X has latent Z_x, Y has latent Z_y, with Z_y = standardized(Z_x^3).

text:
Input dimensions: X=128, Y=128
Train pairs: 160
Held-out pairs: 800
textPreview: Input dimensions: X=128, Y=128 | Train pairs: 160 | Held-out pairs: 800

text:
Latent dimension: 1
Signal strength: 2.0
Noise std: 1.0
textPreview: Latent dimension: 1 | Signal strength: 2.0 | Noise std: 1.0

text: Linear backbone plus controlled nonlinear correction
textPreview: Linear backbone plus controlled nonlinear correction

text: Both views use matched encoder families; residual models use normalized branches so alpha has a meaningful scale.
textPreview: Both views use matched encoder families; residual models use normalized branches so alpha has a meaningful scale.

text: g(u) = Normalize(G u) + alpha * Normalize(A1 GELU(A2 u + b))
textPreview: g(u) = Normalize(G u) + alpha * Normalize(A1 GELU(A2 u + b))

text: Each nonlinear encoder uses one hidden layer: 128 -> 16 -> 4. Embedding dimension is fixed at 4.
textPreview: Each nonlinear encoder uses one hidden layer: 128 -> 16 -> 4. Embedding dimension is fixed at 4.

text: Paper contrastive loss with optional penalties
textPreview: Paper contrastive loss with optional penalties

text: All model families use the same objective; differences come from architecture and regularization.
textPreview: All model families use the same objective; differences come from architecture and regularization.

text:
For a batch of paired embeddings:
sᵢⱼ = zₓ,ᵢᵀ zᵧ,ⱼ
textPreview: For a batch of paired embeddings: | sᵢⱼ = zₓ,ᵢᵀ zᵧ,ⱼ

text: maximize   L = Σᵢ sᵢᵢ − Σᵢ log Σⱼ exp(sᵢⱼ) − Σⱼ log Σᵢ exp(sᵢⱼ)
textPreview: maximize   L = Σᵢ sᵢᵢ − Σᵢ log Σⱼ exp(sᵢⱼ) − Σⱼ log Σᵢ exp(sᵢⱼ)

text: training minimizes  −L / n  plus any active regularization terms
textPreview: training minimizes  −L / n  plus any active regularization terms

text: Available to keep the actual nonlinear correction small unless needed.
textPreview: Available to keep the actual nonlinear correction small unless needed.

text: High train separation with low held-out separation indicates memorization rather than signal.
textPreview: High train separation with low held-out separation indicates memorization rather than signal.

text: In noise-only data, X and Y have no real relationship. All models can still separate paired training samples, so the training alignment reflects memorized noise pairings. The alpha=1 nonlinear model shows the strongest training separation, while held-out separation stays near zero for all models.
textPreview: In noise-only data, X and Y have no real relationship. All models can still separate paired training samples, so the training alignment r...

text: High training alignment in noise does not mean real learning; held-out performance reveals memorization.
textPreview: High training alignment in noise does not mean real learning; held-out performance reveals memorization.

text: Breaking the pairing should remove alignment; values near zero support the sanity check.
textPreview: Breaking the pairing should remove alignment; values near zero support the sanity check.

text: This is a sanity check for noise-only data. Since X and Y are independent, true held-out pairs should not be more meaningful than shuffled pairs. Both original held-out separation and shuffled-pair separation stay close to zero.
textPreview: This is a sanity check for noise-only data. Since X and Y are independent, true held-out pairs should not be more meaningful than shuffle...

text: True held-out pairs behave like shuffled random pairs, confirming that no real signal is learned.
textPreview: True held-out pairs behave like shuffled random pairs, confirming that no real signal is learned.

text: The linear baseline should already be competitive when Z_y = Z_x.
textPreview: The linear baseline should already be competitive when Z_y = Z_x.

text: With linear signal, held-out true-pair separation is positive for most models, so they capture shared structure that generalizes. The linear baseline is already competitive, shuffled values are smaller, and alpha=1 performs poorly.
textPreview: With linear signal, held-out true-pair separation is positive for most models, so they capture shared structure that generalizes. The lin...

text: When the true relationship is linear, the linear baseline is strong and a dominant nonlinear branch does not necessarily help.
textPreview: When the true relationship is linear, the linear baseline is strong and a dominant nonlinear branch does not necessarily help.

text: Correlation with the true latent shows whether embeddings preserve the synthetic signal.
textPreview: Correlation with the true latent shows whether embeddings preserve the synthetic signal.

text: This plot measures how much true latent signal remains in the embeddings. Moderate nonlinear contribution and the L2-regularized nonlinear model improve X-side recovery, while alpha=1 hurts recovery. Y-side recovery is more uneven.
textPreview: This plot measures how much true latent signal remains in the embeddings. Moderate nonlinear contribution and the L2-regularized nonlinea...

text: Moderate nonlinear correction can improve latent recovery, but too much nonlinear influence can hurt even for linear signal.
textPreview: Moderate nonlinear correction can improve latent recovery, but too much nonlinear influence can hurt even for linear signal.

text: The cubic relationship asks whether the nonlinear correction helps beyond the linear backbone.
textPreview: The cubic relationship asks whether the nonlinear correction helps beyond the linear backbone.

text: The cubic setting tests whether nonlinear correction helps beyond the linear backbone. Some controlled nonlinear or regularized models improve held-out separation over the shuffled baseline, suggesting partial nonlinear structure recovery. The alpha=1 model generalizes poorly.
textPreview: The cubic setting tests whether nonlinear correction helps beyond the linear backbone. Some controlled nonlinear or regularized models im...

text: Controlled nonlinear corrections can help nonlinear signal, but an overly large nonlinear branch can destabilize learning.
textPreview: Controlled nonlinear corrections can help nonlinear signal, but an overly large nonlinear branch can destabilize learning.

text: The recovery plot asks whether embeddings capture the true nonlinear latent relationship.
textPreview: The recovery plot asks whether embeddings capture the true nonlinear latent relationship.

text: Latent recovery is less consistent than in the linear setting, showing the cubic relationship is harder to capture. Controlled nonlinear or regularized models preserve more latent information for some views, while alpha=1 damages the representation.
textPreview: Latent recovery is less consistent than in the linear setting, showing the cubic relationship is harder to capture. Controlled nonlinear ...

text: Nonlinear structure is harder to recover; controlled nonlinear correction helps more than a dominant nonlinear branch.
textPreview: Nonlinear structure is harder to recover; controlled nonlinear correction helps more than a dominant nonlinear branch.

text: The measured nonlinear/linear branch ratio checks whether alpha controls the correction size.
textPreview: The measured nonlinear/linear branch ratio checks whether alpha controls the correction size.

text: This diagnostic checks whether alpha controls the nonlinear correction size. Because both branches are normalized before summing, the nonlinear-to-linear ratio should track alpha: alpha=0.01 is tiny, alpha=0.10 is controlled, and alpha=1 is nearly as large as the linear branch.
textPreview: This diagnostic checks whether alpha controls the nonlinear correction size. Because both branches are normalized before summing, the non...

text: Branch normalization makes alpha interpretable: small alpha gives a controlled correction, while alpha=1 lets the nonlinear branch dominate.
textPreview: Branch normalization makes alpha interpretable: small alpha gives a controlled correction, while alpha=1 lets the nonlinear branch dominate.

text: The cleaned experiment separates signal recovery from noise fitting.
textPreview: The cleaned experiment separates signal recovery from noise fitting.

text:
Noise-only: high train alignment but held-out near zero means memorization.
Linear signal: the linear baseline is strong; moderate nonlinear correction can improve recovery.
Cubic signal: controlled or regularized nonlinear models help more than alpha=1.
textPreview: Noise-only: high train alignment but held-out near zero means memorization. | Linear signal: the linear baseline is strong; moderate nonl...

text: The train/test alignment gap is still large in the linear and cubic settings. Branch normalization makes alpha interpretable, but it does not by itself solve overfitting or loss-design issues.
textPreview: The train/test alignment gap is still large in the linear and cubic settings. Branch normalization makes alpha interpretable, but it does...

text:
Understand and reduce the train/test alignment gap for linear and cubic signals.
Try different contrastive loss functions.
Tune the early stopping mechanism and stopping criteria.
Try other signal relationships beyond linear and cubic.
Repeat the strongest settings across more random seeds.
